site.name: RV Appliance Code Atlas
site.safetyBoundary: Use this atlas to identify likely meaning and prepare for service. Shut off power, LP gas, fuel, or water when a listed appliance condition involves flame, gas smell, exhaust, generator shutdown, high temperature, moving jacks/slides, refrigerant, 120 VAC, control boards, repeated ignition failure, or a persistent lockout. Call the appliance maker, RV dealer, or a qualified RV technician for repair.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 9330-33 manual says Er displays when ambient temperature is outside the thermostat display range or when a remote temperature sensor has a problem.
ownerSafeActions: Compare the thermostat display with room conditions.
Record the affected zone and whether Er appears continuously.
Avoid changing wiring or internal thermostat settings.
serviceOnlyActions: Remote temperature sensor circuit, zone control board, signal wiring, and rooftop HVAC service should be performed by trained technicians.
entries.safetyBoundary: Disconnect power before thermostat or HVAC service. Do not open rooftop electrical compartments unless qualified.

ownerSafeActions: Compare the thermostat display with room conditions.
Record whether HI appears continuously or only during heat exposure.
Avoid changing wiring or internal thermostat settings.
serviceOnlyActions: Sensor faults, thermostat wiring, PTC fuse issues, and HVAC control diagnosis belong to trained technicians.
entries.safetyBoundary: Disconnect power before thermostat or HVAC service. Do not probe control wiring unless qualified.
entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach digital thermostat manual says HI displays when current temperature is above 99 F (37 C).
The Coleman-Mach digital thermostat manual says LO displays when current temperature is below 33 F (0 C).
ownerSafeActions: Compare the thermostat display with room conditions.
Record whether LO appears continuously or only at startup.
Avoid changing wiring or internal thermostat settings.
serviceOnlyActions: Sensor faults, thermostat wiring, PTC fuse issues, and HVAC control diagnosis belong to trained technicians.
entries.safetyBoundary: Disconnect power before thermostat or HVAC service. Do not probe control wiring unless qualified.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 9630 heat pump thermostat guides say ELEC flashes when the heat pump is in lockout because electric heat could not satisfy the thermostat call.
ownerSafeActions: Record thermostat model, zone, displayed word, mode, room temperature, set point, outside temperature estimate, and elapsed lockout time.
Wait through the manual-described lockout period if the coach stays safe and warm enough.
After the lockout clears, keep the electric-heat set point within four degrees of room temperature before trying again.
Use normal backup heat controls during the lockout if available.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat installation, wiring, base fuse, jumper configuration, 12 V electrical diagnosis, heat pump, compressor, furnace, and control-circuit work is trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Stop electric heat attempts and call Coleman-Mach/Airxcel or qualified RV HVAC service if lockout repeats, the coach cannot be kept warm, or there are electrical symptoms.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 6535-335 thermostat instructions say DIFF flashes on the LCD when 2nd stage heat is operating because the heat pump cannot satisfy the thermostat.
ownerSafeActions: Record thermostat model, displayed word, mode, room temperature, set point, outside temperature estimate, and elapsed lockout time.
Wait through the manual-described lockout period if the coach stays safe and warm enough.
After the lockout clears, keep the electric-heat set point within four degrees of room temperature before trying again.
Use normal backup heat controls during the lockout if available.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat installation, wiring, base fuse, jumper configuration, 12 V electrical diagnosis, heat pump, compressor, furnace, and control-circuit work is trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Stop electric heat attempts and call Coleman-Mach/Airxcel or qualified RV HVAC service if lockout repeats, the coach cannot be kept warm, or there are electrical symptoms.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 6536-335 thermostat instructions say DIFF displays when backup heat is operating and the heat pump is locked out.
ownerSafeActions: Record thermostat model, displayed word, mode, room temperature, set point, outside temperature estimate, and elapsed lockout time.
Wait through the manual-described lockout period if the coach stays safe and warm enough.
After the lockout clears, keep the electric-heat set point within four degrees of room temperature before trying again.
Use normal backup heat controls during the lockout if available.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat installation, wiring, base fuse, jumper configuration, 12 V electrical diagnosis, heat pump, compressor, furnace, and control-circuit work is trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Stop electric heat attempts and call Coleman-Mach/Airxcel or qualified RV HVAC service if lockout repeats, the coach cannot be kept warm, or there are electrical symptoms.

entries.plainMeaning: Coleman-Mach's heat pump thermostat guide says DIFF can show during the two hour lockout after three heat-pump strikes, with electric heat unavailable during that lockout.
ownerSafeActions: Record thermostat model, displayed word, mode, room temperature, set point, outside temperature estimate, and elapsed lockout time.
Wait through the manual-described two hour lockout if the coach stays safe and warm enough.
After the lockout clears, keep the electric-heat set point within four degrees of room temperature before trying again.
Use normal backup heat controls during the lockout if available.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat installation, wiring, base fuse, jumper configuration, 12 V electrical diagnosis, heat pump, compressor, furnace, and control-circuit work is trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Stop electric heat attempts and call Coleman-Mach/Airxcel or qualified RV HVAC service if lockout repeats, the coach cannot be kept warm, or there are electrical symptoms.

entries.plainMeaning: Coleman-Mach's heat pump thermostat guide says FLASHING GAS HEAT can show during the two hour lockout after three heat-pump strikes, with electric heat unavailable during that lockout.
ownerSafeActions: Record thermostat model, displayed word, mode, room temperature, set point, outside temperature estimate, and elapsed lockout time.
Wait through the manual-described two hour lockout if the coach stays safe and warm enough.
After the lockout clears, keep the electric-heat set point within four degrees of room temperature before trying again.
Use normal backup heat controls during the lockout if available.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat installation, wiring, base fuse, jumper configuration, 12 V electrical diagnosis, heat pump, compressor, furnace, and control-circuit work is trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Stop electric heat attempts and call Coleman-Mach/Airxcel or qualified RV HVAC service if lockout repeats, the coach cannot be kept warm, or there are electrical symptoms.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 9420-330 guide lists a solid green status LED as the device being connected to the network and communicating properly.
ownerSafeActions: Record the LED activity only if it is visible without tools.
Record thermostat model, zone, and whether normal touchscreen controls still respond.
If the thermostat is not operating normally, stop the affected heating or cooling call from normal controls and contact qualified service.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat removal, wiring, zone control box, network communication hardware, installation, and power diagnosis are trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Do not take the thermostat off the wall or disturb thermostat/network hardware unless qualified.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 9420-330 guide lists an off status LED as the device having no power or having completely failed.
ownerSafeActions: Record the LED activity only if it is visible without tools.
Record thermostat model, zone, and whether normal touchscreen controls still respond.
If the off LED state persists, stop the affected heating or cooling call from normal controls and contact qualified service.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat removal, wiring, zone control box, network communication hardware, installation, and power diagnosis are trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Do not take the thermostat off the wall or disturb thermostat/network hardware unless qualified.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 9420-330 guide lists a solid red status LED as the device being offline and not connected to the network.
ownerSafeActions: Record the LED activity only if it is visible without tools.
Record thermostat model, zone, and whether normal touchscreen controls still respond.
If the red LED state persists, stop the affected heating or cooling call from normal controls and contact qualified service.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat removal, wiring, zone control box, network communication hardware, installation, and power diagnosis are trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Do not take the thermostat off the wall or disturb thermostat/network hardware unless qualified.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 9420-330 guide lists a fast flashing green status LED at 4 times per second as the device attempting to make its initial network connection.
ownerSafeActions: Record the LED activity only if it is visible without tools.
Record thermostat model, zone, and whether normal touchscreen controls still respond.
If the fast flashing LED state persists, stop the affected heating or cooling call from normal controls and contact qualified service.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat removal, wiring, zone control box, network communication hardware, installation, and power diagnosis are trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Do not take the thermostat off the wall or disturb thermostat/network hardware unless qualified.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 9420-330 guide lists a slow flashing green status LED at 1 time per second as the device having been online but having no valid network message for 5 seconds.
ownerSafeActions: Record the LED activity only if it is visible without tools.
Record thermostat model, zone, and whether normal touchscreen controls still respond.
If the slow flashing LED state persists, stop the affected heating or cooling call from normal controls and contact qualified service.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat removal, wiring, zone control box, network communication hardware, installation, and power diagnosis are trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Do not take the thermostat off the wall or disturb thermostat/network hardware unless qualified.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 9420-330 guide lists alternating red and orange as the device being offline and attempting to reconnect within 30 seconds.
ownerSafeActions: Record the LED activity only if it is visible without tools.
Record thermostat model, zone, and whether normal touchscreen controls still respond.
If that LED state persists, stop the affected heating or cooling call from normal controls and contact qualified service.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat removal, wiring, zone control box, network communication hardware, installation, and power diagnosis are trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Do not take the thermostat off the wall or disturb thermostat/network hardware unless qualified.

entries.plainMeaning: The Coleman-Mach 9420-330 guide lists alternating green and orange as the device being currently online but having gone offline 2 or more times.
ownerSafeActions: Record the LED activity only if it is visible without tools.
Record thermostat model, zone, and whether normal touchscreen controls still respond.
If that LED state persists, stop the affected heating or cooling call from normal controls and contact qualified service.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat removal, wiring, zone control box, network communication hardware, installation, and power diagnosis are trained technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Use normal thermostat controls only. Do not take the thermostat off the wall or disturb thermostat/network hardware unless qualified.

entries.plainMeaning: The DF furnace manual identifies one flash with a pause as a limit switch or airflow problem.
ownerSafeActions: Turn the furnace off.
Check that interior registers and exterior intake/exhaust openings are unobstructed.
Do not restart if vents were covered, soot is present, or the furnace smells unusual.
serviceOnlyActions: Limit-switch, airflow switch, burner, board, or combustion diagnosis is service technician work.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not operate with blocked vents. Shut off the furnace and LP gas if flame, soot, heat, or exhaust conditions look wrong.

entries.plainMeaning: The Dometic RM1350 operating instructions list E0 as: No communication between the display and power module.
ownerSafeActions: Turn the refrigerator off if the error repeats.
Record the displayed code, model, product number, and serial number.
Keep food safety in mind if cooling is interrupted.
serviceOnlyActions: Display-to-power-module communication diagnosis should be handled by a Dometic dealer or Service Center.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not bypass refrigerator controls, safety devices, LP components, refrigerant/cooling-unit parts, or electrical circuits. Shut off the appliance and arrange qualified service for repeated, unsafe, or unresolved faults.

entries.plainMeaning: The Dometic RM1350 operating instructions list E1 as: Disconnected LP gas valve or LP gas hardware fault; the refrigerator may still be operational on normal electric mode.

serviceOnlyActions: LP valve, wiring, and gas-operation hardware diagnosis should be handled by a Dometic dealer or Service Center.

serviceOnlyActions: Cooling-unit and refrigerant-system diagnosis is service-only work.

entries.plainMeaning: The Dometic RM1350 operating instructions list E4 as: DC voltage is outside the manual range of approximately 8-18 VDC.
ownerSafeActions: Turn the refrigerator off if the error repeats.
Record the displayed code, model, product number, and serial number.
Keep food safety in mind if cooling is interrupted.
serviceOnlyActions: DC power, wiring, converter, and voltage-drop diagnosis should be handled by qualified RV electrical service or a Dometic Service Center.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not bypass refrigerator controls, safety devices, LP components, refrigerant/cooling-unit parts, or electrical circuits. Shut off the appliance and arrange qualified service for repeated, unsafe, or unresolved faults.

entries.plainMeaning: The official Dometic RUA support page lists 01 as a refrigerator fault message. The page states that multiple faults can be present and that the last fault is shown until acknowledged.
ownerSafeActions: Record the exact fault code and take a photo of the display.
Check only owner-safe basics such as RV power state and exterior vent obstruction.
Turn the refrigerator off if cooling is abnormal, the alarm repeats, or the same code returns.
serviceOnlyActions: Internal cooling, LP, electrical, sensor, communication, and control diagnosis belongs to an authorized Dometic service provider.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not bypass refrigerator safety controls or work on LP/electrical components. Shut off LP and power if there is gas odor, scorching, abnormal heat, or repeated fault behavior.

entries.plainMeaning: The official Dometic RUA support page for display fault 01 says to reset the error with the encoder button and continue to authorized service if the problem remains.
ownerSafeActions: Record display fault 01 and take a photo of the display.
Reset the error once by pressing the encoder button for 3 seconds until a beep is heard.
Do not keep holding the encoder button after the reset beep; the page warns that holding too long turns the refrigerator off without resetting the error.
serviceOnlyActions: A continuing display fault 01 must be handled by an authorized service provider.
Internal refrigerator diagnosis, LP hardware, electrical circuits, cooling-system work, wiring, sensors, and control electronics are service-only.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not service refrigerator LP hardware, electrical circuits, wiring, sensors, cooling components, or control electronics; use only the front-panel reset and arrange qualified service if the fault remains or returns.

entries.plainMeaning: The official Dometic RUA support page for display fault 05 says to reset the error with the encoder button and, if the fault remains, check AC supply because voltage is low.
ownerSafeActions: Record display fault 05 and take a photo of the display.
Reset the error once by pressing the encoder button for 3 seconds until a beep is heard.

serviceOnlyActions: A continuing display fault 05 must be handled by an authorized service provider.
AC supply diagnosis, breakers, wiring, voltage testing, sensors, and refrigerator control electronics are service-only.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not service AC circuits, breakers, wiring, sensors, or refrigerator controls; use only owner-visible AC supply status and front-panel reset before qualified service.

entries.plainMeaning: The official Dometic RUA support page for display fault 10 says to make sure the door is properly closed, then reset the error with the encoder button if necessary.
ownerSafeActions: Record display fault 10 and take a photo of the display.
Make sure the refrigerator door is properly closed.
If needed, reset the error once by pressing the encoder button for 3 seconds until a beep is heard.
serviceOnlyActions: A continuing display fault 10 must be handled by an authorized service provider.
Door-switch, latch, wiring, sensor, control-electronics, and internal refrigerator diagnosis are service-only.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not adjust door switches, latches, wiring, sensors, or refrigerator controls; use only normal door closure and front-panel reset checks before arranging qualified service.

entries.plainMeaning: The official Dometic RUA support page for display fault 11 says to reset the error with the encoder button and continue to authorized service if the problem remains.
ownerSafeActions: Record display fault 11 and take a photo of the display.
Reset the error once by pressing the encoder button for 3 seconds until a beep is heard.
Do not keep holding the encoder button after the reset beep; the page warns that holding too long turns the refrigerator off without resetting the error.
serviceOnlyActions: A continuing display fault 11 must be handled by an authorized service provider.
Internal refrigerator diagnosis, LP hardware, electrical circuits, cooling-system work, wiring, sensors, and control electronics are service-only.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not service refrigerator LP hardware, electrical circuits, wiring, sensors, cooling components, or control electronics; use only the front-panel reset and arrange qualified service if the fault remains or returns.

entries.plainMeaning: The official Dometic RUC support page lists E03 as a refrigerator fault message. The page explains that warning-type faults reset after the condition clears and error-type faults require manual reset after the cause is corrected.
ownerSafeActions: Record the exact fault code and take a photo of the display.
Check only obvious owner-safe conditions such as blocked exterior vents or interrupted RV power.
Turn the refrigerator off if cooling is abnormal and arrange model-specific service.
serviceOnlyActions: Cooling-system, LP, electrical, sensor, control, and repeated-fault diagnosis belongs to an authorized Dometic service provider.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not bypass refrigerator safety controls or work on LP/electrical components. Shut off LP and power if there is gas odor, scorching, abnormal heat, or repeated fault behavior.

Check battery voltage only from owner-visible RV monitor panels or normal refrigerator controls.
Reset the error once by pressing the encoder button for 3 seconds until a beep is heard.
serviceOnlyActions: A continuing E33 fault must be handled by an authorized service provider.
Battery-supply diagnosis, wiring, fuses, sensors, and control electronics are service-only.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not service DC circuits, wiring, fuses, sensors, or refrigerator controls; use only owner-visible battery-voltage checks and front-panel reset before qualified service.
partCaptureHints: battery voltage shown by owner-visible monitor

entries.plainMeaning: The official Dometic RUC support page for display fault E34 says to make sure the refrigerator was not filled with warm goods, then reset the error with the encoder button.
ownerSafeActions: Record display fault E34 and take a photo of the display.
Check whether the refrigerator was recently filled with warm goods.
Reset the error once by pressing the encoder button for 3 seconds until a beep is heard.
serviceOnlyActions: A continuing E34 fault must be handled by an authorized service provider.
Cooling-system, compressor, sensor, wiring, and control-electronics diagnosis are service-only.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not service refrigerator cooling components, compressor, wiring, sensors, or controls; use only owner-visible loading and front-panel reset checks before qualified service.

entries.plainMeaning: The official Dometic RUC support page for display fault E35 says to make sure refrigerator ventilation is not obstructed, notes that extreme ambient temperatures can trigger the over-temperature code, and says remaining repair requires an authorized service provider.
ownerSafeActions: Record display fault E35 and take a photo of the display.
Check only owner-visible refrigerator ventilation openings for obstruction.
Note ambient temperature and reset the error once by pressing the encoder button for 3 seconds until a beep is heard.
serviceOnlyActions: A continuing E35 over-temperature fault must be handled by an authorized service provider.
Cooling-system, compressor, airflow-system, sensor, wiring, and control-electronics diagnosis are service-only.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not remove refrigerator panels, service airflow components, wiring, sensors, compressor/cooling components, or controls; use only owner-visible ventilation checks and front-panel reset before qualified service.
partCaptureHints: ambient temperature and visible ventilation status

entries.plainMeaning: The Dometic Single Zone LCD thermostat manual lists E1 as loss of communication between the Single Zone LCD thermostat and the module board; the LCD cycles between E1 and the previous mode setting, and the system shuts down.
ownerSafeActions: Record the displayed code, previous mode, setpoint, and selected fan setting.
Turn the thermostat off from the normal On/Off Mode button if the HVAC equipment is not responding.
Note whether normal RV shore power, generator power, or battery power is present from installed indicators.
serviceOnlyActions: Single Zone thermostat communication and module-board diagnosis should be handled by qualified RV HVAC service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not open thermostat, rooftop HVAC, or electrical compartments, and do not bypass communication or sensor circuits. Shut the affected HVAC system off and arrange qualified RV HVAC service for unresolved Single Zone LCD faults.
partCaptureHints: air conditioner or heat pump model number
photo of LCD error code and mode LED

entries.plainMeaning: The Dometic Single Zone LCD thermostat manual lists E2 as an open circuit or out-of-range indoor temperature sensor; heating and cooling operation lock out while fan operation can continue.
ownerSafeActions: Record the E2 code, room temperature, mode, fan setting, and setpoint.
Use only normal thermostat controls to turn off heat or cooling if the coach is uncomfortable.
Note whether fan-only operation continues before calling service.
serviceOnlyActions: Indoor temperature sensor and thermostat circuit diagnosis should be handled by qualified RV HVAC service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not open thermostat, rooftop HVAC, or electrical compartments, and do not bypass communication or sensor circuits. Shut the affected HVAC system off and arrange qualified RV HVAC service for unresolved Single Zone LCD faults.
partCaptureHints: air conditioner or heat pump model number
photo of LCD error code and mode LED

entries.plainMeaning: The Dometic Single Zone LCD thermostat manual lists E3 as a shorted indoor temperature sensor; heating and cooling operation lock out while fan operation can continue.

Use only normal thermostat controls to turn off heat or cooling if the coach is uncomfortable.

entries.plainMeaning: The Dometic Single Zone LCD thermostat manual lists E4 as an open circuit or out-of-range outdoor temperature sensor on select models; heat pump operation locks out while air conditioner, fan, and furnace operation can continue.
ownerSafeActions: Record the E4 code, selected mode, fan setting, setpoint, and outdoor temperature estimate.

Avoid repeated heat-pump retries if the E4 code returns.
serviceOnlyActions: Outdoor temperature sensor and heat-pump control diagnosis should be handled by qualified RV HVAC service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not open thermostat, rooftop HVAC, or electrical compartments, and do not bypass communication or sensor circuits. Shut the affected HVAC system off and arrange qualified RV HVAC service for unresolved Single Zone LCD faults.
partCaptureHints: air conditioner or heat pump model number

ownerSafeActions: Record the E5 code, selected mode, last setpoint, and whether cooling had been running.
Turn cooling off from normal thermostat controls if the air conditioner is locked out.

serviceOnlyActions: Freeze-sensor, evaporator-protection, and air-conditioner diagnosis should be handled by qualified RV HVAC service.

entries.plainMeaning: The Dometic CCC2 operating instructions list E1 as: Loss of communication between the CCC2 thermostat and all or one system power module board; all-board loss shuts the system down and one-zone loss displays the affected zone.
ownerSafeActions: Record the affected zone number and displayed code.
Turn the affected HVAC equipment off if it is not operating normally.
Use normal RV power indicators to note whether shore power, generator power, or battery power is present.
serviceOnlyActions: CCC2 communication, module-board, and zone wiring diagnosis should be handled by qualified RV HVAC service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not bypass thermostat wiring, rooftop equipment controls, module boards, DIP switches, 120 VAC circuits, or freeze/sensor safeties. Shut off the affected HVAC equipment and arrange qualified RV HVAC service for unresolved faults.

entries.plainMeaning: The red lockout lamp indicates the water heater has a no-heat fault or failed operation condition on Dometic WH-series LP gas water heaters.
ownerSafeActions: Turn the water heater off.
Confirm the tank is full before any restart attempt.
Write down whether the appliance was in gas or electric mode.
serviceOnlyActions: Persistent lockout, overheating, ECO, thermal cut-off, flame, gas, or wiring diagnosis requires Dometic service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not open gas components, bypass the ECO, or keep resetting an overheating water heater. Shut off LP and power and call service if the lockout persists.

entries.plainMeaning: The Furrion refrigerator manual lists E1 as voltage on compressor is too low or too high.

Stop use if voltage appears abnormal.
serviceOnlyActions: Sensor, fan, compressor, inverter, communication wiring, model-mismatch, and sealed-system diagnostics should be handled by Furrion support or a qualified RV refrigerator technician.
entries.safetyBoundary: Disconnect power before refrigerator service. Do not open electrical compartments, compressor wiring, inverter wiring, fans, sensors, or refrigerant systems unless qualified.

ownerSafeActions: Stop operating the slide.
Check battery state from an installed monitor.
Record whether the fault happened during travel or movement.
serviceOnlyActions: Controller reset, motor synchronization, encoder checks, wiring repair, motor service, and manual override should follow Lippert documentation or qualified service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not place hands or tools near moving slide mechanisms. Keep people clear and do not force the slide if current, short, or controller faults appear.
entries.plainMeaning: The Lippert In-Wall Slide-out troubleshooting manual maps Red LED 2 to Battery Drop Out: battery capacity low enough to drop below 6 V DC while running or a short in switch wiring.

ownerSafeActions: Stop operating the slide.
Check battery state from an installed monitor.
Charge the RV battery before retrying if safe.
serviceOnlyActions: Controller reset, motor synchronization, encoder checks, wiring repair, motor service, and manual override should follow Lippert documentation or qualified service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not place hands or tools near moving slide mechanisms. Keep people clear and do not force the slide if current, short, or controller faults appear.
entries.plainMeaning: The Lippert In-Wall Slide-out troubleshooting manual maps Red LED 3 to Low Battery: voltage below 8 V DC at the start of cycle.

ownerSafeActions: Stop operating the slide.
Record charger or battery monitor readings before calling service.
serviceOnlyActions: Controller reset, motor synchronization, encoder checks, wiring repair, motor service, and manual override should follow Lippert documentation or qualified service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not place hands or tools near moving slide mechanisms. Keep people clear and do not force the slide if current, short, or controller faults appear.
entries.plainMeaning: The Lippert In-Wall Slide-out troubleshooting manual maps Red LED 4 to High Battery: voltage greater than 18 volts.

ownerSafeActions: Stop operating the slide.
Keep people and objects clear of the slide room.
Do not force the room if one side stalls.
serviceOnlyActions: Controller reset, motor synchronization, encoder checks, wiring repair, motor service, and manual override should follow Lippert documentation or qualified service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not place hands or tools near moving slide mechanisms. Keep people clear and do not force the slide if current, short, or controller faults appear.
entries.plainMeaning: The Lippert In-Wall Slide-out troubleshooting manual maps Red LED 5 to Excessive Motor Current: high amperage, also indicated by one side of the slide continually stalling.

ownerSafeActions: Stop operating the slide.
Do not retry repeatedly after a short-circuit fault.
serviceOnlyActions: Controller reset, motor synchronization, encoder checks, wiring repair, motor service, and manual override should follow Lippert documentation or qualified service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not place hands or tools near moving slide mechanisms. Keep people clear and do not force the slide if current, short, or controller faults appear.
entries.plainMeaning: The Lippert In-Wall Slide-out troubleshooting manual maps Red LED 6 to Motor Short Circuit: motor or wiring to motor has shorted out.

ownerSafeActions: Stop operating the slide.
Record green LED motor blinks and red LED count for service.
serviceOnlyActions: Controller reset, motor synchronization, encoder checks, wiring repair, motor service, and manual override should follow Lippert documentation or qualified service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not place hands or tools near moving slide mechanisms. Keep people clear and do not force the slide if current, short, or controller faults appear.
entries.plainMeaning: The Lippert In-Wall Slide-out troubleshooting manual maps Red LED 8 to Wire Short Between Controller and Motor: encoder is not providing a signal, usually a wiring problem.

entries.plainMeaning: The Suburban furnace guide describes lockout after the furnace fails to ignite after multiple attempts.

serviceOnlyActions: Ignition, combustion, flame-sense, gas, and board troubleshooting is qualified service.

entries.plainMeaning: The Suburban tank water heater guide describes red RESET light lockout after ignition attempts fail.

serviceOnlyActions: Ignition, flame, gas valve, electrode, soot, combustion, and board work is qualified service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not cycle the water heater repeatedly after failed ignition. Shut off LP/power and call service if lockout returns.

entries.plainMeaning: The RM3762/RM3962 operating instructions list E0 as no communication between the display and power module.
ownerSafeActions: Record the flashing E0 display and model information before any reset.
Use the front ON/OFF control only after service has corrected the communication fault.
serviceOnlyActions: Display-to-power-module communication diagnosis and module or harness repair belong with a Dometic dealer or qualified RV service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not open electrical compartments or diagnose refrigerator controls; document E0 and arrange qualified service.

entries.plainMeaning: The RM3762/RM3962 operating instructions list E1 as a disconnected LP valve or hardware fault in the LP operation system; the refrigerator may still operate on 120 V AC.
ownerSafeActions: If there is any gas odor, shut LP off, ventilate, evacuate, and call service.
If AC operation is available from the front panel, use that mode only while arranging service.
serviceOnlyActions: LP operation hardware, ignition, and refrigerator control diagnosis are service-only.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not service LP hardware or attempt LP operation repairs; use a safe alternate mode only if available and call qualified service.

entries.plainMeaning: The RM3762/RM3962 operating instructions list E2 as a failure of the temperature sensor device or associated electronic circuitry.
ownerSafeActions: Record the E2 display and monitor food temperature.
Stop using the refrigerator if safe temperatures cannot be maintained.
serviceOnlyActions: Temperature sensor and electronic-circuit diagnosis or replacement should be handled by a Dometic dealer or qualified service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Do not access sensor wiring or refrigerator electronics; preserve the code and schedule qualified service.

entries.plainMeaning: The RM3762/RM3962 operating instructions list E3 as a cooling unit malfunction.
ownerSafeActions: Turn the refrigerator off and protect food if cooling has stopped.
If you smell ammonia or see leakage, ventilate and do not use the refrigerator.
serviceOnlyActions: Cooling-unit inspection, replacement, or leak diagnosis is service-only.
entries.safetyBoundary: A cooling-unit malfunction is not owner repairable; stop use when cooling is unsafe and contact a Dometic service center.

entries.plainMeaning: The official Furrion electric fireplace manuals list display EE as a thermostat sensor broken or disconnected condition.
ownerSafeActions: Record the EE display, fireplace model, and whether heat or fan output was present.
Turn the fireplace off from normal controls and disconnect coach power to the fireplace before service.
Stop using the fireplace until Lippert, Furrion, or qualified RV electrical service checks the thermostat sensor fault.
serviceOnlyActions: Thermostat sensor connection, back-panel access, main circuit board checks, and sensor replacement belong with Lippert, Furrion, or qualified RV electrical service.

entries.plainMeaning: The official Furrion electric fireplace manuals list display 88 as manual reset overheat protection triggered.
ownerSafeActions: Turn the fireplace off from normal controls and let it cool completely.
Confirm visible air inlets and outlets are clear and soft goods are at least 6 inches away.
Restart once with the normal power button after cooling; stop using the fireplace if 88 returns.
serviceOnlyActions: Repeated overheat protection, heater inspection, sensor diagnosis, and internal electrical service belong with Lippert, Furrion, or qualified RV electrical service.
entries.safetyBoundary: Disconnect power before fireplace service. Internal heater, sensor, and qualified RV electrical work are not owner troubleshooting steps.

symptoms.title: RV refrigerator is not cooling
symptoms.summary: Use this when an absorption or compressor refrigerator shows a stopped-cooling, high-temperature, no co, or similar cooling fault.
safeChecklist: Move food if temperature is unsafe.
Record brand, model, code, power source, outside temperature, and whether vents are blocked.
Do not bypass recall, high-temperature, or cooling-unit safety devices.

symptoms.title: RV refrigerator or gas appliance will not ignite on LP
symptoms.summary: Use this for no FL, flame-sense, or ignition failures on LP appliances.
safeChecklist: Shut off LP if you smell gas.
Do not hand-light appliances with electronic ignition.
Record whether the problem happens only on LP mode.

symptoms.title: RV water heater has no heat or lockout light

symptoms.title: RV furnace locks out or will not ignite

symptoms.title: Pre-service checklist for RV appliance calls

safeChecklist: Brand, model, serial number.
Exact code and photo of display.

symptoms.title: RV rooftop air conditioner is not cooling enough

Confirm cooling mode, lower set temperature, and open supply nozzles or vents.
Remove visible leaves or dirt from exterior grilles only from normal safe access.
Record whether the compressor delay, freeze sensor, or fan state changed before calling service.

symptoms.title: RV rooftop air conditioner leaks water inside
symptoms.summary: Use this when a rooftop air conditioner allows condensation or meltwater into the RV living space.

symptoms.title: Coleman-Mach rooftop AC not cooling temperature split

safeChecklist: Clean or replace owner-accessible filters if the manual allows it.
Set HIGH COOL from normal controls and let the unit run 15-20 minutes before measuring.
Use the same thermometer at the return-air filter and the first supply register, then record outdoor temperature and power source.
Call Coleman-Mach, the dealer, or qualified RV HVAC service if the split is outside 16-22 degrees or cooling stays weak.

symptoms.title: Coleman-Mach 47000 AC cooling and power check

symptoms.title: Coleman-Mach 47000 heat pump freezing and auxiliary heat limits
symptoms.summary: The Coleman-Mach 47000 heat-pump owner manual says the heat pump shuts down near freezing to prevent outdoor coil freeze-up, auxiliary resistance heat is only a chill chaser and not a furnace substitute, and repeated high-pressure lockouts need qualified service.

If cooling airflow drops from coil icing, stop cooling and run HIGH FAN until full airflow returns before retrying.
Clean owner-accessible filters and record outdoor temperature, mode, airflow, and whether auxiliary heat was expected.
Call qualified RV HVAC service if heat-pump shutdowns, high-pressure lockouts, or weak heating repeat in moderate weather.

symptoms.title: Coleman-Mach 49000 AC high-pressure lockout

symptoms.title: Furrion furnace will not light with blower not turning on
symptoms.summary: The official Furrion RV Furnaces Troubleshooting and Service Manual lists owner checks for power reset, LP supply, fuse-panel status, low 12 V DC, thermostat OFF for 10 seconds then ON, and blocked intake or vents before trained RV service handles breaker or persistent no-light conditions.
safeChecklist: Turn the thermostat off, wait 10 seconds, and turn Heat back on once; stop repeated reset attempts if lockout returns.
If there is no gas odor, confirm LP supply valves, tank status, thermostat Heat setting, coach 12 V status, fuse panel status, and blocked intake or vents.

symptoms.title: RV thermostat delay or no response
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Dometic or Coleman-Mach/Airxcel thermostat appears asleep, blinks an HVAC state, waits before compressor operation, or a zone does not respond to settings.
safeChecklist: Confirm the thermostat mode, zone, fan setting, and setpoint allow the requested operation.
Wake the display and wait through the built-in communication or short-cycle delay.
Record whether the display is dim, blinking, remote-sensor controlled, or zone-specific.
Do not change upper control-board settings or thermostat wiring.

symptoms.title: Coleman-Mach thermostat shows GAS or flashing Elec assist
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Coleman-Mach thermostat indicates backup gas heat is assisting or electric heat is temporarily locked out after repeated unmet heat calls.
safeChecklist: Treat GAS or flashing Elec as backup heat behavior described by the thermostat manual.
Record outdoor temperature, heat mode, zone, and how long the heat call has been running.
Wait through the manual-described lockout period if the coach remains safe and warm enough.
Do not change upper control-board settings or heat wiring.

symptoms.title: Coleman-Mach thermostat heat-pump lockout display
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Coleman-Mach heat-pump thermostat shows ELEC flashing, DIFF, DIFF flashing, or FLASHING GAS HEAT after repeated unmet electric-heat calls.
safeChecklist: Record thermostat family, zone, display text, heat mode, room temperature, set point, outside temperature estimate, and how long the lockout has been active.
Wait through the manual-described lockout period if the RV remains safe and warm enough.
After lockout clears, keep the electric-heat set point within four degrees of room temperature before trying electric heat again.
Use normal backup heat controls during the lockout if available, and contact qualified service if lockout repeats in moderate outdoor conditions.

symptoms.title: Coleman-Mach 9420-330 thermostat status LED pattern
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Coleman-Mach 9420-330 multi-zone thermostat status LED shows off, red, flashing green, or alternating red/orange or green/orange network states.
safeChecklist: Record the LED activity only if it is visible without tools.
Record thermostat model, zone, touchscreen state, and whether heating or cooling responds from normal controls.
Stop the affected heating or cooling call from normal controls if the thermostat does not respond normally.
Contact qualified RV HVAC service for persistent abnormal status LED patterns.

symptoms.title: Dometic/Atwood water heater lockout light
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Dometic/Atwood tank water heater fails to ignite, lights the red lockout lamp, or has slow recovery in gas/electric operation.
safeChecklist: Turn the water heater mode switch off, then wait before another start attempt.
Confirm normal LP supply and water-heater mode selection from the RV controls only.
Stop repeated reset attempts if lockout returns or slow recovery continues.
Call a qualified RV appliance technician for ignition, thermostat, ECO, or wiring diagnosis.

symptoms.title: Dometic water heater overheat lockout reset
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Dometic water heater is in an over-heating lockout and support/manual guidance points to cooling and reset at the switch.
safeChecklist: Turn the water heater off and let the water cool before any reset attempt.
Use only the wall switch or normal control switch reset described by Dometic support.
Do not keep resetting an overheat lockout that returns.
Call service for repeated overheat lockout, thermostat, ECO, or hot-water temperature problems.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 startup or lockout behavior
symptoms.summary: Use this when an OD-5001 on-demand water heater will not start normally, needs first-use air purge, fails ignition, or lights the optional lockout lamp.
safeChecklist: Pressurize the water system and purge air from hot-water lines from normal faucets.
Confirm 12V power and LP supply using normal RV controls only.
Reset ignition only with the water-heater power switch after one failed start attempt.
Turn the appliance off and call service if overheating occurs, gas does not shut off, or lockout returns.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 power vent fan not running
symptoms.summary: Use this when water flow is on but the OD-5001 heater does not come on and the power vent fan is not running.
safeChecklist: Turn the water heater off if it does not start normally.
Record whether water flow was present and whether the fan was silent.
Check only normal RV power controls and breaker state from the user panel.
Do not open the appliance or control enclosure; call service for internal fuse or board checks.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 rapidly cycles on and off
symptoms.summary: Use this when an OD-5001 burner starts but cycles rapidly during hot-water use.
safeChecklist: Open the hot-water outlet enough to keep steady flow through the heater.
Clean faucet aerators or shower screens only where they are owner-accessible.
Avoid operating the heater below the minimum flow described by Dometic support.
Call service if rapid cycling continues after restoring normal water flow.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 temperature fluctuates during use
symptoms.summary: Use this when an OD-5001 burner runs but hot-water temperature fluctuates erratically.
safeChecklist: Reduce outlet restrictions by cleaning owner-accessible showerhead screens, faucet aerators, or strainers.
Keep hot-water flow steady and avoid partially restricted fixtures during testing.
Stop use if water temperature becomes unsafe or unpredictable.
Call service if temperature swings continue after normal outlet restrictions are cleared.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 low heat rise or excessive flow needed to trigger
symptoms.summary: Use this when an OD-5001 needs excessive flow to trigger, has low heat rise, or behaves like cold water is bleeding into the hot line.
safeChecklist: Close unused hot and cold faucets or valves during troubleshooting.
Do not rely only on a showerhead pause button to stop water flow.
Close the actual shower valves after use so cold water cannot refill the hot line.
Call a qualified technician if low heat rise or excessive trigger flow continues.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 no temperature control, too-hot water, or too-low water at low flow
symptoms.summary: Use this when an OD-5001 has no normal temperature control, water is too hot, or water is too cool at lower flow rates.
safeChecklist: Stop use if water temperature is unsafe or unpredictable.
Confirm the summer/winter setting matches incoming-water temperature using only owner-accessible controls.
Keep flow steady during testing so the heater is not chasing changing demand.
Call service if temperature control stays wrong after normal control checks.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 burner ignites but water temperature is too low
symptoms.summary: Use this when the OD-5001 burner lights but hot water is still too cool during use.
safeChecklist: Slow hot-water flow if supply water is very cold and the heater is at capacity.
Keep one fixture flowing steadily while checking temperature.
Stop use if temperature becomes unsafe or unstable.
Call service if the burner lights but the water remains too cool.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 showerhead pause causes cold-water purge
symptoms.summary: Use this when using a showerhead pause button or navy-shower pattern causes cold water to purge through the line before hot water returns.
safeChecklist: Run the shower continuously during troubleshooting so flow through the heater stays steady.
Avoid cycling only the showerhead pause button when checking the heater.
Close the actual hot and cold shower valves when finished.
Call service if cold-water purges continue during steady normal flow.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 winter operation and winterizing
symptoms.summary: Use this when preparing an OD-5001 for freezing storage or deciding what to record before freeze-risk service.
safeChecklist: Turn the water heater off and shut off LP from normal RV controls before winterizing.
Follow Dometic and coach winterizing steps exactly for draining, air, or RV antifreeze.
Do not operate a frozen or suspected freeze-damaged water heater.
Call service before use if the unit froze or leaks after winter storage.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 gas smell
symptoms.summary: Use this when gas odor is present near a Dometic OD-5001 water heater or inside the RV.
safeChecklist: Leave the RV and keep people away from ignition sources.
Shut off LP at the source only if it is safe to do so.
Do not touch switches, radios, phones, engines, generators, or flames in the RV.
Contact the gas supplier, a qualified RV technician, or the fire department before restoring LP.

symptoms.title: Dometic XT water heater has low flow or cold flow
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Dometic XT tank water heater has low hot-water flow, cold flow, or not-hot-enough water from the XT mixing-valve condition described in the official manual.
safeChecklist: Stop use if hot-water temperature is unsafe or unpredictable.
Record the XT model number, symptom, tank maintenance history, and whether the issue is low flow or cold water.
Do not remove or service the XT mixing valve.
Call a certified RV appliance technician for mixing-valve service or persistent XT cold-flow symptoms.

symptoms.title: Dometic water heater gas smell
symptoms.summary: Use this when gas odor is present near a Dometic water heater or in the RV.
safeChecklist: Evacuate people from the RV.
Shut off LP at the container or source only if it is safe to do so.
Do not touch switches, radios, phones, generators, engines, or ignition sources in the RV.
Call the gas supplier, a qualified RV technician, or the fire department before restoring LP.

symptoms.title: Dometic water heater rotten-egg odor
symptoms.summary: Use this for unpleasant rotten-egg or hydrogen-sulfide odor from a Dometic tank water heater's hot water.
safeChecklist: Confirm the odor is from hot water, not an LP leak; use the gas-smell guide if gas odor is possible.
Turn off the water heater before draining or flushing.
Do not add aftermarket anode rods or non-approved devices to Dometic/Atwood aluminum tank water heaters.
Use only owner-accessible tank draining or flushing steps if you are comfortable with them.
Call service if odor persists, fittings are not owner-accessible, or water-heater service is needed.

symptoms.title: Dometic water heater soot, delayed ignition, or lazy flame
symptoms.summary: Use this when Dometic maintenance guidance points to soot, delayed ignition, ignition outside the combustion area, or a flame appearance change.
safeChecklist: Turn the water heater off.
Keep the exterior vent area clear from normal safe access.
Do not clean or adjust combustion parts as an owner troubleshooting step.
Call a qualified RV appliance technician before operating again.

symptoms.title: Suburban tankless water heater lockout
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban IW60, IW60RL, ST-42, or ST-60 on-demand water heater fails ignition trials, enters lockout, or times out after extended operation.
safeChecklist: Turn the faucet or control switch off, then back on once using the normal reset path described by Suburban.
Open the hot-water faucet fully during startup so the unit has enough flow.
Stop repeated reset attempts if lockout returns.
Call Suburban, a dealer, or a qualified RV technician for repeated ignition or lockout problems.

symptoms.title: Suburban tankless water heater low flow or temperature swing
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban tankless water heater is slow to deliver hot water, needs minimum flow, or drops temperature because flow is restricted.
safeChecklist: Open the hot-water faucet enough to meet the manual's minimum flow requirement.
Do not use a showerhead pause button during troubleshooting because the manual says reduced flow can shut the heater down.
Remove owner-accessible faucet or shower flow restrictions only if safe and allowed by the coach setup.
Call service if low-flow or temperature swings continue.

symptoms.title: Suburban IW60RL recirculation delay or pump noise
symptoms.summary: Use this when an IW60RL recirculation system cycles, delays hot water, or has a loud recirculation pump after air enters the lines.
safeChecklist: Confirm whether recirculation is enabled on the On-Demand Control Center.
Run hot water at multiple faucets for the manual-described air purge if the pump is loud.
Record whether recirculation is enabled or disabled and whether hot lines cool between uses.
Call service if pump noise or delayed hot water persists.

symptoms.title: Furrion tankless water heater low flow or low temperature
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Furrion tankless water heater has low hot-water flow, weak heat, cold-water mixing, hard-water scale, or slow temperature rise.
safeChecklist: Confirm LP, water, and 12V power are on from normal RV controls.
Check that shower and bypass valves are closed so cold water is not bleeding into the hot side.
Clean owner-accessible faucet screens or inlet filters only if the manual allows it and you can do it without disturbing gas or electrical parts.
Call Furrion or a qualified technician for persistent low temperature, gas-volume, altitude, or internal service concerns.

symptoms.title: Furrion water heater pressure relief discharge or drain tray water
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Furrion tankless water heater has water at the pressure relief outlet, drain hose, drainage tray, or inlet filter.
safeChecklist: Turn the water heater off if water is leaking or discharging unexpectedly.
Confirm the RV water pressure is within the manual's allowed range using installed or external pressure regulation.
Do not plug, cap, or bypass any relief outlet.
Call Furrion or service if discharge repeats, the tray has water, or the pressure-relief part needs replacement.

symptoms.title: Furrion tankless water heater freeze mode or freeze damage
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Furrion tankless water heater shows freeze-protection behavior, Fd winter-use mode, or visible freeze-damage/leak symptoms after improper winterization.
safeChecklist: Shut off water, LP, and power if the heater is leaking or visibly damaged.
Do not rely on freeze-protection mode to winterize the entire RV plumbing system.
Photograph visible damage, the display state, model label, and winterization history for warranty or service.
Contact Lippert/Furrion or a qualified RV technician before operating a suspected freeze-damaged heater.

symptoms.title: Furrion tankless water heater filter cleaning or descaling maintenance
symptoms.summary: Use this when Furrion tankless guidance points to owner maintenance for the water filter, hard-water scale, or descaling before service.
safeChecklist: Turn the water heater off before owner-accessible filter or descaling maintenance.
Use only the Furrion/Lippert maintenance procedure and chemicals allowed for the model.
Do not open gas, electrical, blower, valve, or controller areas.
Call Lippert/Furrion or a qualified technician if flow or temperature does not recover after allowed maintenance.

symptoms.title: Girard GSWH-2 low flow or temperature swing
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Girard GSWH-2 has weak hot-water flow, water not hot enough, water too hot or cold, low campground pressure, filter restriction, or temperature swing.
safeChecklist: Confirm LP, water, and 12V power are on from normal RV controls and the set temperature is reasonable.
Try the onboard pump if campground pressure is low, and clean owner-accessible faucet strainers or inlet screens only when the manual allows it.
Keep shower and fixture handles fully closed between uses so cold water does not bleed through the hot side.
Call Lippert/Girard or a qualified RV technician for repeated codes, persistent low temperature, low voltage, LP supply, or internal service concerns.

symptoms.title: Girard GSWH-2 filter screen or decalcify maintenance
symptoms.summary: Use this when Girard GSWH-2 guidance points to owner-accessible filter-screen cleaning, decalcifying, or visual maintenance before service.
safeChecklist: Turn the water heater off before owner-accessible filter, screen, or decalcifying work.
Use only the Lippert/Girard maintenance procedure and materials allowed for the model.
Do not open appliance service compartments or disturb gas, electrical, blower, valve, or controller parts.
Call Lippert/Girard or a qualified technician if flow, temperature, or visible condition does not recover after allowed maintenance.

symptoms.title: Girard GSWH-2 winterization or freeze-leak risk
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Girard GSWH-2 is in winter-use mode, shows freeze-leak symptoms, or may have been improperly winterized.
safeChecklist: Shut off water, LP, and 12V power if the heater is leaking or visibly damaged.
Do not rely on winter-use mode or an Fd display to winterize the whole RV plumbing system.
Photograph visible damage, the display state, model label, and winterization history for warranty or service.
Contact Lippert/Girard or a qualified RV technician before operating a suspected freeze-damaged heater.

symptoms.title: Girard GSWH-2 gas smell
symptoms.summary: Use this when gas odor is present near a Girard GSWH-2 water heater or inside the RV.
safeChecklist: Leave the RV and keep people away from ignition sources.
Shut off LP at the source only if it is safe to do so.
Do not touch switches, radios, phones, engines, generators, or flames in the RV.
Contact the gas supplier, Lippert/Girard, a qualified RV technician, or the fire department before restoring LP.

symptoms.title: Girard GSWH-2 no hot water or lockout
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Girard GSWH-2 does not heat, shuts down, locks out, or displays a control-panel fault.
safeChecklist: Confirm the user control panel, exterior on/off control, water supply, LP, and 12V power are on from normal RV controls.
Turn the hot-water faucet off and back on once only if the manual describes that reset for the displayed condition.
Stop repeated restart attempts if the heater locks out, overheats, smells like gas, leaks, or shows the same code again.

symptoms.title: Girard GSWH-1/GSWH-1M LED diagnostics or lockout
symptoms.summary: Use this when a legacy Girard GSWH-1 or GSWH-1M diagnostic LED is steady on, blinking one through five flashes, or the heater enters lockout.

Stop repeated restart attempts and record LP, 12V, water-flow, and model-label details.
Use Lippert/Girard or qualified RV service for internal control, airflow, flame, low-voltage, or repeated lockout faults.

symptoms.title: Girard GSWH-1/GSWH-1M low flow, temperature swing, or limiting
symptoms.summary: Use this when a legacy Girard tankless water heater has low flow, temperature swings, limiting/cycling, or water that is too hot or not hot enough.

Follow the exact manual for the model: GSWH-1 uses hot-water flow and mode settings, while GSWH-1M uses gas-modulation and flow adjustments.
Check owner-accessible faucet or shower restrictions only when the model manual allows it, and keep exterior louvers visibly clear.

symptoms.title: Girard GSWH-1/GSWH-1M winterization or freeze-risk
symptoms.summary: Use this when a legacy Girard GSWH-1 winter-use device, GSWH-1M built-in winter mode, storage winterization, or suspected freeze damage is involved.
safeChecklist: Winterize the whole RV plumbing before storage or freezing weather; the winter-use device protects only the water heater internal piping.
For intended winter operation, verify only normal owner-visible conditions: fresh water, 12V power, water pump, LP supply, and the heater switched on.
Shut off water, LP, and 12V power for leaks, suspected freeze damage, gas odor, or visible damage.
Treat winter-use-device installation, thermostat, solenoid, wiring, and leak repair as qualified service work.

symptoms.title: Girard GSWH-1/GSWH-1M gas smell
symptoms.summary: Use this when gas odor is present near a legacy Girard GSWH-1 or GSWH-1M water heater or inside the RV.

Do not touch switches, radios, phones, engines, generators, or flames in the RV.
Contact the gas supplier, Lippert/Girard, a qualified RV technician, or the fire department before restoring LP.

symptoms.title: Dometic water heater winterizing or tank flushing
symptoms.summary: Use this when preparing a Dometic/Atwood tank water heater for freezing storage, flushing sediment, or recording a winterizing issue before service.

symptoms.title: Dometic water heater P/T relief dripping or lost air gap
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Dometic/Atwood tank water heater drips from the pressure-temperature relief outlet and Dometic air-gap guidance may apply.

Do not cap, plug, restrict, or ignore the P/T relief outlet.
Call qualified service if discharge repeats, the relief valve is corroded, or any valve or plumbing part needs replacement.

symptoms.title: Dometic OD-5001 no water flow from tap
symptoms.summary: Use this when an OD-5001 installation has no water flow from the tap after the tap is turned on.

symptoms.title: Suburban furnace blows cold air, locks out, or hits limit
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban gas furnace fan runs but heat does not start, the furnace enters lockout, or the FAQ points to a limit-switch condition.

Do not keep cycling the thermostat after repeated no-heat or no-ignition attempts.

Record the thermostat setting, number of start attempts, blower run time, and whether heat ever reached the vents for a dealer or Suburban service center.

Remove only visible return-air restrictions and loose items near the furnace compartment.

symptoms.title: Suburban SF-VH furnace duct-port layout, limit cycling, or weak airflow
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban SF-35VH(F)Q or SF-42VH(F)Q furnace short-cycles, runs hot, or has weak heat because the duct-port layout may not match Suburban's SF-VH ducting guidance; the guide notes port #5 has little effect on limit temperature, port #2 or port #3 may matter more, and the SF-42 Bottom Duct Kit 520753 can apply to some layouts.

symptoms.title: Suburban SF-VH return-air, register, or flex-duct airflow layout
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban SF-VH furnace has rapid cycling, weak heat, overheating, or airflow complaints tied to minimum open duct area through registers, return-air restriction, a heat outlet within 18 inches blowing toward the return, crushed or sharply bent flex duct, loose duct connections, or added air boosters.
safeChecklist: Keep outlet registers open and aim adjustable outlets away from the return-air opening if they blow directly into it.
Remove only loose visible items blocking return-air grilles or furnace compartment airflow.

symptoms.title: Suburban SF-VH thermostat location or premature satisfaction
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban SF-VH furnace shuts off before the coach feels warm and the thermostat may be mounted too high, on the wrong wall, too far from the return grate, or otherwise satisfied before the living space reaches set temperature.
safeChecklist: Record thermostat height, wall location, nearby heat sources, and whether the furnace stops before the living area warms.
Compare the thermostat location to Suburban guidance: central, near the return grate, on an inside wall, and about 4-1/2 feet above the floor.

symptoms.title: Suburban tank water heater RESET light or ignition lockout

symptoms.title: Suburban tank water heater P/T relief drip or lost air pocket

symptoms.summary: Use this when hot water from a Suburban tank water heater smells like sulfur or the anode rod needs inspection, draining, or replacement planning.

symptoms.title: Suburban tankless freeze protection or voltage protection
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban IW60, IW60RL, ST-42, or ST-60 on-demand water heater has freeze-protection behavior, winterizing concerns, or low/high 12V protection.
safeChecklist: Do not rely on freeze protection as a substitute for winterizing the entire RV water system.
Drain or winterize the system before storage in freezing temperatures according to the Suburban and RV instructions.
Record whether gas and electrical power were available, whether the unit dripped water in cold weather, and whether coach voltage was below 10VDC or above 17VDC.
Call qualified service for repeated voltage protection, freeze-related leaks, pressure-relief discharge, or any work behind a service panel.

symptoms.title: Suburban ST42 cold-inlet, winter-use, or not-hot-enough complaint

safeChecklist: Confirm whether the water heater label is ST42 or ST60 and record the set temperature and approximate incoming-water temperature.
Open hot water enough to meet the ST manual minimum flow, then reduce flow or mix cold only after hot water stabilizes.
Treat ST42 cold-inlet or winter underperformance as a model-capacity and service-planning issue, not an internal repair.
Call Suburban, the RV manufacturer, or qualified service for repeated low temperature, LP supply, voltage protection, or installation concerns.

symptoms.title: Suburban range or cooktop gas odor
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban range or cooktop area has smell gas or propane odor; Suburban range and cooktop guides say to avoid ignition sources, evacuate, shut off propane at the container or source when safe, call the gas supplier or fire department, and have the condition corrected by qualified service.
safeChecklist: Do not try to light any appliance.
Extinguish open flames, evacuate everyone, and avoid switches, phones, radios, the engine, and the generator.
Shut off LP at the container or source only if it is safe from outside the odor area.
Call the gas supplier or fire department and use qualified service before restoring gas.

symptoms.title: Suburban cooktop burner lighting, air in line, or blowout
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban cooktop burner will not light normally, takes extra time after air in the gas line, or blowout happens; Suburban says delayed ignition can occur after air in the gas line, to light only one burner at a time, and to turn the knob OFF, wait five minutes, and relight only if no gas odor remains.
safeChecklist: Identify the correct knob before lighting and keep the cover fully open.
Light only one burner at a time and stay with the appliance while it is on.
If the flame extinguishes or blows out, turn the knob OFF and wait five minutes before one normal relight attempt.
If odor remains or the burner repeatedly will not stay lit, stop use and contact qualified service.

safeChecklist: Turn the affected knob OFF and stop using that burner.
Keep the cover fully open and the area clear of loose items while documenting the symptom.
Record flame color, whether cabinet doors or drafts affect it, and whether the knob is hard to turn.
Use qualified service if yellow flame, repeated no-light, failure to stay lit, or difficult knob movement continues.

symptoms.title: Suburban range oven pilot, vent, and carbon monoxide boundary

safeChecklist: Before any pilot-light attempt, turn controls OFF, open the oven door, and stop if gas odor is present.
Keep oven vent openings and airflow paths uncovered, and do not line the oven bottom or rack with foil.
Never use the oven, range, or cooktop as a space heater.
Use qualified service if the pilot will not stay lit, odor is present, or covered-vent operation occurred.

symptoms.title: Suburban griddle ignition, grease, or storage safety
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban griddle does not ignite within 5 seconds, grease or oil catches fire, or storage is planned; Suburban says to turn controls off and wait 5 minutes after no ignition, not use water on a grease fire, and store the griddle only after it is off, cool, and at room temperature.
safeChecklist: If ignition does not occur within 5 seconds, turn the control OFF and wait 5 minutes before another normal attempt.
Do not lean over the griddle while lighting.
For a grease fire, turn burners and gas source off only if safe, call the fire department, and do not use water.
Let the griddle cool before moving or storage; store it only when off, disconnected from the low-pressure gas source, and at room temperature.

symptoms.title: Suburban griddle flame check, venturi, or valve service boundary

safeChecklist: Before use, observe only the owner-visible flame behavior described by the manual.
If flame behavior is abnormal, the knob is not smooth, the visible hose looks damaged, or insects or blockage are suspected, turn the griddle OFF and stop use.
Keep ventilation openings and the cooking area clear.
Use an RV service center or qualified service before operating again.

symptoms.title: Suburban electric wall heater thermostat and high-limit shutdown
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban electric wall heater still has the switch light on, restarts later from thermostat demand, shuts down on high-limit overheat, or has blocked air inlet or outlet flow; Suburban says the heater cannot be completely turned off by use of the thermostat knob alone, high-limit switches shut it down if overheated, and the end user may not service or modify it.
safeChecklist: To fully turn the heater off, use the ON/OFF switch; thermostat position alone is not a full shutdown.
For hard-wired units, turn controls off and turn power to the heater circuit off at the main disconnect before cleaning.
Keep air inlets, outlets, and nearby combustibles clear; if high limit trips, remove only visible blockages and let the heater cool at least 10 minutes.
Do not service or modify the heater as an end user; contact a technician or Customer Service if the problem remains.

symptoms.title: Suburban induction cooktop shows no pan or incompatible cookware
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban single or double element induction cooktop shows E0 or will not heat with the current pan.
safeChecklist: Turn the cooktop off before changing cookware.
Use cookware marked for induction or cookware that a magnet sticks to, with contents inside and centered on the element.
Record the model, serial, and stock numbers if E0 continues with compatible cookware.
Contact Suburban Customer Service or qualified RV service if the display error continues.

symptoms.title: Suburban induction cooktop low or high voltage error
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban induction cooktop displays E1 or E2 and RV power quality may be involved.
safeChecklist: Turn the cooktop off and stop cooking until RV power is stable.
Record whether the code appears on shore power, generator power, inverter power, or a specific campsite pedestal.
Check only normal RV power status indicators and whether other appliances show power problems from owner-accessible controls.
Call qualified RV electrical service for repeated E1 or E2, damaged cords or plugs, or suspected outlet or supply problems.

symptoms.title: Suburban induction cooktop surface or internal circuit too hot
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban induction cooktop displays E3 or E5 and needs cooldown before reuse.
safeChecklist: Turn the cooktop off.
Wait for the surface to be completely cool before cleaning or restarting.
After cooldown, confirm cookware was not empty and the cooktop has clear space for fan ventilation.
Contact Suburban Customer Service or qualified RV service if the overheat code repeats.

symptoms.title: Suburban induction cooktop sensor or internal circuit error
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Suburban induction cooktop displays E4, E6, or E7 and Suburban service guidance is needed.
safeChecklist: Turn the cooktop off.
Record the displayed code plus the model, serial, and stock numbers from the ratings label.
Contact Suburban Customer Service, the dealer, or qualified RV service.
Do not use the cooktop again after a repeated internal error until it has been evaluated by service.

symptoms.title: Norcold absorption refrigerator display fault codes
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Norcold or Thetford absorption refrigerator shows N3000 service-mode codes, N10LX/2118 alternating display codes, legacy N-series light/display faults, LP ignition faults, AC/DC supply faults, or backup-operation thermistor warnings.
safeChecklist: Photograph the exact display wording or service-mode code before resetting anything.
Record model, serial number, power mode, LP state, battery state, and whether an audible alarm is present.
Do not open refrigerator electrical compartments, adjust LP parts, or bypass safety devices.

symptoms.title: Norcold DC compressor refrigerator LED diagnostics
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Norcold N2000, N8DCX, or N10DCX refrigerator shows a red/orange LED state, user-interface fault code, or compressor power-module flash count.
safeChecklist: Record the LED color, flash count, displayed code, and whether cooling stopped.
Check normal owner-visible battery monitor readings before calling service.
Do not inspect wiring, fuses, compressor modules, fans, or thermistor circuits unless qualified.

symptoms.title: Norcold legacy refrigerator stopped-cooling lockout
symptoms.summary: Use this when an older Norcold absorption refrigerator shows no co or n/stopped-cooling style lockout wording. Record the first event, check owner-visible airflow, level, and door state, and treat repeat lockouts as qualified-service issues.

Check that vents are not blocked, doors are closed, the RV is level, and food temperature is safe.
Do not keep resetting a stopped-cooling lockout; a repeat event is a service call.

symptoms.title: Norcold absorption refrigerator not cooling, level, or ventilation check

If a no co, n, warning light, gas smell, ammonia smell, or repeated warm condition appears, shut the refrigerator off and arrange qualified Norcold/Thetford service.

symptoms.title: Norcold absorption refrigerator power-source or startup behavior

Use qualified service if the refrigerator still will not start, respond, or select an available source.

symptoms.title: Norcold absorption refrigerator not cooling well on 120 V AC

symptoms.title: Norcold refrigerator door alarm, sagging door, or seal frost

Use qualified Norcold service for a suspected switch, sagging door, warped door, or seal correction.

symptoms.title: Norcold refrigerator frost, condensation, or defrost safety

norcold condensation frost service
safeChecklist: Move food to a cooler, turn the refrigerator off, open the doors, and place towels for melt water.
Let frost melt naturally; do not use a hair dryer, heat gun, boiling water, or sharp tool.
Dry the interior before restarting and reduce repeat frost by closing doors quickly and covering liquids.
Arrange qualified service if frost or condensation returns quickly after normal defrost and door checks.

symptoms.title: Norcold refrigerator odor or cleaning
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Norcold/Thetford refrigerator has ordinary storage odor or needs owner-safe cleaning guidance, while separating gas, ammonia, or chemical smells into service boundaries.

safeChecklist: Turn the refrigerator off and clean accessible interior surfaces with a mild household cleaner.
For ordinary storage odor, use documented odor absorbers such as vanilla water, charcoal, wet newspaper, or baking soda.
Dry the interior and leave doors open when storing the refrigerator.
If the smell suggests gas, ammonia, burning plastic, or chemicals, shut the refrigerator off, ventilate, and arrange qualified service.

symptoms.title: Norcold refrigerator temperature setting is too warm or too cold
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Norcold/Thetford owner is trying to identify the coldest setting or adjust a refrigerator that is too warm or too cold, while keeping model-specific scales separate.

safeChecklist: Identify the exact model before assuming the setting scale because older and newer Norcold controls differ.
For models documented with 1 to 5 settings, use 5 as coldest and 1 as warmest; for other controls, follow that model manual.
Adjust one step at a time and allow the cabinet temperature to stabilize before changing it again.
Move food and arrange service if safe food temperature cannot be maintained or the refrigerator overcools on the warmest setting.

symptoms.title: Norcold high-temperature sensor light or stopped-operation recall boundary
symptoms.summary: Use this when a Norcold refrigerator stopped operating with high-temperature sensor wording, solid red HTS light, or flashing HTS light. The owner-safe result is shutdown, documentation, and authorized Norcold service.

Contact a Norcold Authorized Service Center for the HTS condition.

symptoms.title: Norcold DC compressor refrigerator cooling or voltage symptom

For high ambient heat, ventilate the vehicle, clear only visible ventilation blockage, and use day mode when available.
Close doors, keep interior airflow clear, defrost excess ice, and arrange qualified service if cooling remains poor or the compressor runs continuously.

symptoms.title: Norcold DC compressor refrigerator too cold, night mode, or noise

For high ambient heat or repeated start delay, ventilate the vehicle and use day mode when available.
Arrange qualified service if noise is abnormal, cooling is unstable, or the compressor behavior remains constant after owner checks.

symptoms.title: Norcold N2000 condensation, water, or drip tray

Arrange qualified service if water appears outside normal condensation or returns after defrost and door checks.

symptoms.title: Furrion 12V refrigerator not cooling or hard reset

symptoms.title: Furrion refrigerator not cold enough, seals, vents, or temperature

symptoms.title: Furrion 12V refrigerator compressor cycling, low battery, or heat

Use qualified service if compressor cycling repeats after battery, door, ventilation, and ambient-temperature checks.

symptoms.title: Furrion refrigerator door seal or door not closing

symptoms.title: Furrion refrigerator moisture, ice, condensation, or defrost

symptoms.title: Furrion 12V refrigerator no power, service line, or reset

symptoms.title: Furrion refrigerator temperature mode, lock mode, or off-grid setting

symptoms.title: Furrion refrigerator normal noises, vibration, or rattling

symptoms.title: Furrion refrigerator service-only compressor, sensor, wiring, or refrigerant boundary

symptoms.title: Dometic RM10 ammonia smell
